title: Pakistan called it a terrorist facility. The UN called it a rehab centre. Google Earth still has the before image — 2026-05-13
narrative: On March 16, 2026, Pakistan's air force struck what it described as a 'military and terrorist infrastructure' site in Kabul — a rehabilitation centre for drug addicts. Pakistan claims it targeted militants. The UN and independent investigators say the site was a civilian rehab centre. The BBC reports 269 Afghan deaths; the UN says the toll 'likely exceeded' that figure. Families are demanding answers; a war crimes investigation is being called for.
telemetry: GOOGLE MAPS / GOOGLE EARTH: The rehab centre was publicly visible on Google Maps with a documented civilian address, publicly listed patient capacity, and registered ministry documentation — before and after satellite imagery is available via Google Earth Engine and Sentinel-2 archives

SENTINEL-2 / PLANET LABS: Multi-spectral satellite imagery from before and after March 16, 2026 is publicly accessible — structure type, roof condition, and destruction footprint are verifiable geospatial evidence; Planet Labs archives at 3-5m resolution are searchable by date and coordinates

ISPR COORDINATES VS. REGISTRY: Pakistan's military media (ISPR) published specific target coordinates; cross-referencing those coordinates against the publicly registered Afghan Ministry of Public Health listing for the rehab centre will confirm or contradict the 'terrorist facility' classification

UN BODY COUNT METHODOLOGY: The UN's 'likely exceeded 269' methodology vs. Pakistan's own figures — the UN bases estimates on survivor testimony, satelliteconfirmed structural destruction, and hospital admission records from Afghan health ministry sources; Pakistan has provided no public methodology

AFGHAN MINISTRY OF PUBLIC HEALTH REGISTRATION: The centre was registered as a civilian medical facility with documented address, GPS coordinates, and patient roster — a matter of public record, not disputed by Pakistan, and verifiable via Afghan government registry
analysis: The evidentiary asymmetry here is stark. Pakistan's 'terrorist facility' claim rests on classified intelligence that cannot be independently verified. The countervailing evidence — a publicly registered civilian medical facility with a known address, a visible structure on Google Maps, and satellite imagery showing the destruction — is all public and consistent with a civilian rehab centre destroyed with a large loss of life. The ISPR coordinate claim is directly falsifiable: if those coordinates match the registered rehab centre location, the 'terrorist facility' narrative collapses. The UN's body count methodology is traceable to named sources and documented hospital admissions; Pakistan's counter-figure has no stated methodology. When a government kills 269 civilians and calls them militants, the burden of proof is geospatial — and the evidence needed to meet it is already in the public domain.
verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT] Pakistan called it a terrorist facility. The satellite imagery, the Ministry registration, and the UN's independent methodology all point the other way. The coordinates ISPR published are the starting point — if they match the rehab centre's registered GPS, the civilian classification is proven and the war crimes investigation has its smoking gun.
